title: Disorders of Growth and Development: Clinical Perspectives

relatedChapters.title: Sex-Steroid Priming, GHST & Empty Sella (Case Report)

items.title: Why short stature is defined by height SDS
items.content: Short stature is defined as height < -2 standard deviations (below the 3rd percentile) for age and sex, which standardizes across populations and allows meaningful comparison over time.
keyPoints: Height SDS standardizes age and sex.
Cutoff is below -2 SDS or <3rd percentile.
Serial SDS changes highlight abnormal growth.
items.reference: Short stature: height < -2 SDS for age/sex is the standard definition.

items.title: How growth velocity flags pathology early
items.content: A fall in growth velocity often precedes a drop in height percentile. Measuring height over 6–12 months and comparing to age norms is more sensitive than a single height measurement.
keyPoints: Velocity decline precedes percentile crossing.
Measure over at least 6 months.
Compare to age-specific velocity ranges.
items.reference: Growth velocity: a sustained slowdown is an early marker of disease.

items.title: Why fetal and postnatal growth have different drivers
items.content: Fetal growth is dominated by placental function and IGF-2, while postnatal growth depends on nutrition, thyroid hormone, and the GH–IGF-1 axis. This explains why endocrine disorders present after birth.
keyPoints: Placenta and IGF-2 drive fetal growth.
Postnatal growth depends on GH, IGF-1, and thyroid.
Endocrine causes usually present postnatally.
items.reference: Growth physiology: fetal growth is placenta/IGF-2 driven, postnatal growth is GH–IGF-1 and thyroid dependent.

items.title: How the growth plate controls linear growth
items.content: Chondrocyte proliferation in the resting and proliferative zones, followed by hypertrophy and ossification, drives linear growth. Sex steroids accelerate maturation and eventual epiphyseal fusion.
keyPoints: Growth plate zones determine linear growth.
Chondrocyte hypertrophy is critical for lengthening.
Sex steroids speed maturation and fusion.
items.reference: Growth plate: chondrocyte proliferation and hypertrophy drive height gain, with sex steroids closing plates.

items.title: Why obesity can mimic growth hormone deficiency
items.content: Obesity suppresses spontaneous GH secretion and blunts stimulation testing, yet IGF-1 is often normal. Linear growth is usually preserved or increased, distinguishing it from true GHD.
keyPoints: Obesity suppresses GH secretion.
IGF-1 may remain normal.
Normal or tall stature argues against true GHD.
items.reference: Obesity-GH paradox: low GH secretion with preserved growth differentiates it from true GHD.

items.title: How body proportions guide differential diagnosis
items.content: Upper-to-lower segment ratio and arm span help identify disproportionate short stature. Skeletal dysplasias show shortened limbs, while endocrine or chronic disease is typically proportionate.
keyPoints: Disproportion suggests skeletal dysplasia.
Proportionate short stature favors endocrine/systemic causes.
Arm span < height may indicate limb shortening.
items.reference: Body proportions: disproportion points toward skeletal dysplasia rather than endocrine causes.

items.title: How to calculate midparental (target) height
items.content: Midparental height predicts genetic potential. For boys, add 13 cm to maternal height and average with paternal height; for girls, subtract 13 cm from paternal height and average with maternal height.
keyPoints: Use sex-adjusted midparental height.
Compare child height to target range.
Range of ±8.5 cm (±2 SDS) is typical.
items.reference: Target height: midparental height estimates genetic potential with a ±8.5 cm range.

items.title: How to choose the correct growth chart
items.content: WHO charts are used for children under 5 years, while IAP or country-specific references are preferred for older children. Consistent chart use is essential for tracking percentiles.
keyPoints: Use WHO charts for <5 years.
Use IAP or national charts for older children.
Avoid switching charts mid-follow-up.
items.reference: Growth charts: WHO for <5 years, IAP/national charts for older children.

items.title: Why height SDS is more informative than percentiles
items.content: Height SDS allows precise comparison across ages and identifies small changes over time. Percentiles can mask clinically meaningful shifts when children are already at extremes.
keyPoints: SDS quantifies deviation from mean.
Small SDS shifts may be clinically important.
Percentiles compress data at extremes.
items.reference: Height SDS: a more sensitive metric for growth change than percentiles.

items.title: How bone age refines growth interpretation
items.content: Bone age reflects skeletal maturity and predicts remaining growth. Delayed bone age suggests constitutional delay or endocrine disease, whereas advanced bone age suggests early puberty or androgen excess.
keyPoints: Delayed bone age indicates prolonged growth potential.
Advanced bone age suggests early maturation.
Compare bone age with height age and chronological age.
items.reference: Bone age: skeletal maturity helps distinguish delayed vs advanced growth patterns.

items.title: How to distinguish familial short stature from CDGP
items.content: Familial short stature has normal growth velocity and bone age matching chronological age, whereas constitutional delay shows delayed bone age with later pubertal growth spurts.
keyPoints: FSS: normal bone age and normal timing.
CDGP: delayed bone age and delayed puberty.
Family history guides interpretation.
items.reference: FSS vs CDGP: normal bone age in FSS, delayed bone age in CDGP.

items.title: Why small-for-gestational-age infants need catch-up monitoring
items.content: Most SGA infants catch up by age 2 years. Failure to catch up suggests endocrine or genetic causes and may warrant GH evaluation.
keyPoints: Catch-up usually by 2 years.
Persistent short stature needs evaluation.
GH therapy is considered when catch-up fails.
items.reference: SGA: lack of catch-up growth by age 2 warrants evaluation for treatment.

items.title: Why isolated short stature can be celiac disease
items.content: Celiac disease may present without GI symptoms; malabsorption and inflammation impair growth. Screening with tissue transglutaminase IgA (and total IgA) is recommended.
keyPoints: Short stature may be the only sign.
Screen with tTG-IgA and total IgA.
Growth often improves on a gluten-free diet.
items.reference: Celiac disease: isolated short stature warrants serologic screening.

items.title: How chronic kidney disease impairs growth
items.content: CKD causes metabolic acidosis, poor appetite, and GH resistance, leading to poor linear growth. Optimizing nutrition, correcting acidosis, and rhGH can improve outcomes.
keyPoints: GH resistance is common in CKD.
Acidosis and malnutrition worsen growth.
rhGH may be indicated after stabilization.
items.reference: CKD: GH resistance and metabolic derangements drive growth failure.

items.title: Why renal tubular acidosis causes growth failure
items.content: Chronic acidosis impairs bone mineralization and GH-IGF-1 signaling, leading to poor linear growth. Alkali therapy can normalize growth velocity.
keyPoints: Acidosis disrupts bone and growth factors.
RTA often presents with poor growth.
Alkali therapy can restore growth.
items.reference: RTA: chronic acidosis impairs growth and improves with alkali correction.

items.title: How neonatal clues suggest congenital GHD/MPHD
items.content: Neonatal hypoglycemia, prolonged jaundice, micropenis, or midline defects raise suspicion for congenital GH deficiency or multiple pituitary hormone deficiency.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia and cholestasis are red flags.
Micropenis suggests gonadotropin deficiency.
Midline defects raise structural pituitary concern.
items.reference: Neonatal clues: hypoglycemia, cholestasis, micropenis, and midline defects suggest congenital GHD/MPHD.

items.title: Why IGF-1 must be interpreted with nutrition and age
items.content: IGF-1 is GH dependent but also falls with malnutrition, chronic disease, or liver dysfunction. Low IGF-1 is supportive but not diagnostic of GHD.
keyPoints: IGF-1 is nutrition and age sensitive.
Low IGF-1 supports but does not confirm GHD.
Use alongside growth data and GH testing.
items.reference: IGF-1: low levels are supportive but non-specific for GHD.

items.title: How GH stimulation testing is interpreted
items.content: Because GH is pulsatile, stimulation tests (e.g., clonidine, insulin, glucagon) assess reserve. Poor peak GH supports GHD when clinical data and auxology are consistent.
keyPoints: Random GH levels are not diagnostic.
Use stimulation tests to assess reserve.
Interpret in clinical context.
items.reference: GH stimulation: low peak GH in the right clinical context supports GHD.

items.title: How to monitor children with MPHD on rhGH
items.content: Monitoring includes growth velocity, IGF-1 targets, thyroid function, and adrenal sufficiency. Intercurrent illness and puberty require dose adjustments.
keyPoints: Track growth velocity every 3–6 months.
Check IGF-1 and thyroid function regularly.
Ensure adrenal reserve is protected.
items.reference: MPHD monitoring: growth velocity, IGF-1, and pituitary axes require ongoing assessment.

items.title: Why rhGH can unmask hypothyroidism
items.content: GH therapy increases peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 and can reveal central hypothyroidism by lowering free T4. Thyroid testing should precede and follow rhGH initiation.
keyPoints: rhGH can lower free T4.
Check thyroid function before and after starting GH.
Treat hypothyroidism to optimize growth response.
items.reference: GH therapy: can unmask central hypothyroidism, so thyroid monitoring is required.

items.title: How juvenile hypothyroidism affects growth
items.content: Acquired hypothyroidism causes slowed growth velocity, weight gain, delayed bone age, and delayed puberty. Catch-up growth can occur after levothyroxine therapy.
keyPoints: Growth velocity slows markedly.
Bone age is delayed with weight gain.
Replacement therapy can restore growth.
items.reference: Juvenile hypothyroidism: growth deceleration with delayed bone age and weight gain.

items.title: Why pseudohypoparathyroidism causes short stature
items.content: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy includes short stature, brachydactyly, and relative resistance to PTH and sometimes TSH or GHRH. Skeletal disproportion and obesity help differentiate it.
keyPoints: AHO features include brachydactyly and short stature.
Hormone resistance can include TSH or GHRH.
Look for obesity and rounded facies.
items.reference: Pseudohypoparathyroidism: AHO phenotype with hormone resistance contributes to short stature.

items.title: How to recognize skeletal dysplasia on exam
items.content: Disproportionate limb shortening, macrocephaly, abnormal spine curvature, and joint laxity suggest skeletal dysplasia. Radiographs showing metaphyseal or epiphyseal changes confirm.
keyPoints: Disproportion and macrocephaly are clues.
Spine and limb abnormalities are common.
Radiographs help define the dysplasia.
items.reference: Skeletal dysplasia: disproportion and radiographic changes distinguish from endocrine causes.

items.title: Why bone age helps distinguish endocrine from genetic short stature
items.content: Endocrine disorders often show delayed bone age relative to height age, while genetic syndromes or skeletal dysplasias may have bone age that is not as delayed despite short stature.
keyPoints: Endocrine causes: delayed bone age.
Genetic syndromes: bone age often closer to chronological age.
Interpret with auxology and proportions.
items.reference: Bone age: marked delay suggests endocrine causes, whereas genetic syndromes may show less delay.

items.title: Why to monitor for hip dysplasia or SCFE during rhGH
items.content: Rapid growth and underlying endocrine disorders increase risk for slipped capital femoral epiphysis and hip pain. New limp or hip/knee pain warrants urgent evaluation.
keyPoints: SCFE risk rises with rapid growth.
Hip or knee pain needs urgent assessment.
Hold GH if SCFE is suspected.
items.reference: rhGH safety: rapid growth can precipitate SCFE, so hip symptoms require urgent review.

items.title: How to approach a 9-year-old with MPHD and short stature
items.content: A child with severe short stature, low growth velocity, and multiple pituitary hormone deficiencies needs GH replacement after ensuring adrenal and thyroid axes are secured. Monitor growth response, IGF-1, and orthopedic symptoms.
keyPoints: Secure cortisol and thyroid replacement first.
Start rhGH with close growth monitoring.
Watch for orthopedic complications.
items.reference: MPHD approach: stabilize adrenal/thyroid axes before rhGH and monitor growth plus orthopedic symptoms.

items.title: Why pituitary MRI is important in suspected MPHD
items.content: MRI can identify pituitary stalk interruption, hypoplastic anterior pituitary, or structural midline defects that explain MPHD and guide anticipatory management.
keyPoints: Structural defects explain hormone deficits.
Pituitary stalk interruption is a classic finding.
Imaging guides long-term monitoring.
items.reference: MPHD imaging: pituitary MRI identifies structural anomalies and informs prognosis.

items.title: How chronic undernutrition affects growth
items.content: Calorie and protein deficits reduce IGF-1 and slow linear growth. Nutritional rehabilitation typically restores growth velocity before considering endocrine therapy.
keyPoints: Undernutrition lowers IGF-1.
Growth improves after nutritional recovery.
Evaluate nutrition early in assessment.
items.reference: Nutrition: inadequate intake suppresses IGF-1 and slows linear growth.

items.question: A 9-year-old boy has severe short stature, growth velocity 2 cm/year, low IGF-1, and deficiencies of ACTH and TSH. MRI shows pituitary stalk interruption. What is the next best step before initiating rhGH?
options: Start rhGH immediately to avoid further height loss
Begin hydrocortisone and levothyroxine replacement first
Observe and repeat growth measurements in 6 months
Prescribe high-dose vitamin D and calcium
items.explanation: Adrenal and thyroid replacement must be secured before GH therapy in MPHD.
items.reference: MPHD management: stabilize cortisol and thyroid axes prior to starting rhGH.

items.question: Short stature is best defined as height:
options: Below the 10th percentile for age and sex
Below -2 SDS for age and sex
More than 5 cm below midparental height
Below the 25th percentile for age and sex
items.explanation: The standard definition is height below -2 SDS (approximately <3rd percentile).
items.reference: Definition: short stature is height < -2 SDS for age and sex.

items.question: A 6-year-old girl has height at the 5th percentile but her growth velocity has dropped from 6 cm/year to 3 cm/year. What does this most strongly suggest?
options: Early sign of pathologic growth failure
items.explanation: A sustained decline in growth velocity is an early indicator of disease.
items.reference: Growth velocity: a persistent slowdown is an early marker of pathology.

items.question: Which factor primarily drives fetal linear growth?
items.explanation: Fetal growth is dominated by placental health and IGF-2.
items.reference: Fetal growth: placenta and IGF-2 are key drivers.

items.question: A child with short stature has arm span far shorter than height and an increased upper-to-lower segment ratio. What category of causes is most likely?
items.explanation: Disproportionate short stature suggests skeletal dysplasia.
items.reference: Body proportions: disproportionate limb shortening points toward skeletal dysplasia.

items.question: A boy’s mother is 150 cm and father is 170 cm. What is the midparental height?
items.explanation: For boys: (mother + 13 cm + father) / 2 = (163 + 170)/2 = 166.5 cm.
items.reference: Midparental height: boys add 13 cm to maternal height and average with paternal height.

items.question: Which growth chart is preferred for a 2-year-old child in India?
items.explanation: WHO charts are recommended for children younger than 5 years.
items.reference: Growth charts: WHO standards are used under 5 years.

items.question: A 12-year-old boy has short stature, delayed puberty, and bone age of 9 years. Growth velocity is otherwise normal. What diagnosis fits best?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
items.explanation: Delayed bone age with delayed puberty and normal velocity suggests CDGP.
items.reference: CDGP: delayed bone age with later pubertal timing.

items.question: Which feature favors familial short stature over constitutional delay?
options: Normal bone age for chronological age
items.explanation: FSS typically has normal bone age matching chronological age.
items.reference: FSS: bone age aligns with chronological age.

items.question: An infant born small for gestational age has not shown catch-up growth by age 2 years. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Evaluate for endocrine or genetic causes and consider GH therapy
items.explanation: Lack of catch-up by age 2 warrants evaluation and possible GH therapy.
items.reference: SGA: persistent short stature beyond age 2 requires evaluation.

items.question: A 7-year-old with short stature has no GI symptoms. Which screening test is most appropriate?
options: Tissue transglutaminase IgA with total IgA
items.explanation: tTG-IgA with total IgA is the recommended screening test.
items.reference: Celiac screening: tTG-IgA and total IgA are first-line.

items.question: Which mechanism most contributes to growth failure in chronic kidney disease?
items.explanation: CKD causes GH resistance and metabolic derangements that impair growth.
items.reference: CKD: GH resistance and acidosis drive growth failure.

items.question: A 5-year-old with growth failure has normal renal function but metabolic acidosis and high urine pH. What is the most likely explanation?
items.explanation: RTA causes chronic metabolic acidosis and growth failure.
items.reference: RTA: chronic acidosis with growth failure suggests renal tubular acidosis.

items.question: Which neonatal feature most strongly suggests congenital GH deficiency?
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia and cholestasis are classic neonatal clues to GHD/MPHD.
items.reference: Neonatal clues: hypoglycemia and cholestasis raise suspicion for congenital GHD.

items.question: Which statement best describes the role of IGF-1 in evaluating short stature?
options: Normal IGF-1 rules out GH deficiency
Low IGF-1 is diagnostic of GH deficiency
IGF-1 is supportive but non-specific for GH deficiency
IGF-1 should not be used in children
items.explanation: IGF-1 is helpful but affected by nutrition and chronic disease.
items.reference: IGF-1: supportive but non-specific for GHD.

items.question: Why are GH stimulation tests required to diagnose GH deficiency?
options: GH is secreted in pulses and random levels are unreliable
GH is not affected by sleep
items.explanation: Pulsatile secretion makes random GH levels unreliable.
items.reference: GH testing: stimulation is required because GH is pulsatile.

items.question: A child on rhGH has declining free T4 with normal TSH. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Growth hormone toxicity to the thyroid
items.explanation: rhGH can unmask central hypothyroidism by lowering free T4.
items.reference: GH therapy: can reveal central hypothyroidism with low free T4.

items.question: Which growth pattern best fits juvenile hypothyroidism?
options: Rapid growth with advanced bone age
Slow growth with weight gain and delayed bone age
Normal growth velocity with tall stature
items.explanation: Acquired hypothyroidism causes growth deceleration and delayed bone age.
items.reference: Juvenile hypothyroidism: slowed growth with delayed bone age and weight gain.

items.question: A child with short stature, obesity, brachydactyly, and rounded facies most likely has:
items.explanation: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy features suggest pseudohypoparathyroidism.
items.reference: Pseudohypoparathyroidism: AHO phenotype with short stature and brachydactyly.

items.question: Which feature most strongly indicates skeletal dysplasia rather than endocrine short stature?
options: Delayed bone age with normal proportions
Slow growth velocity with normal proportions
items.explanation: Disproportion and macrocephaly are typical of skeletal dysplasia.
items.reference: Skeletal dysplasia: disproportion and macrocephaly are distinguishing clues.

items.question: A child on rhGH develops a new limp and hip pain. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Stop GH temporarily and evaluate for SCFE
items.explanation: Hip pain in a growing child on GH raises concern for SCFE.
items.reference: rhGH safety: hip pain or limp warrants urgent evaluation for SCFE.

items.question: Which monitoring element is essential for a child with MPHD receiving rhGH?
options: IGF-1, thyroid function, and growth velocity tracking
items.explanation: Growth velocity and pituitary axis monitoring are required.
items.reference: MPHD monitoring: track growth, IGF-1, and thyroid/adrenal axes.

items.question: Why is height SDS preferred over percentiles for longitudinal growth tracking?
options: SDS detects small meaningful shifts over time
items.explanation: SDS quantifies deviations and highlights subtle change.
items.reference: Height SDS: sensitive for detecting growth shifts.

items.question: An obese child has low peak GH on stimulation but normal height velocity. What is the best interpretation?
items.explanation: Obesity can suppress GH secretion without true deficiency.
items.reference: Obesity-GH paradox: low GH with preserved growth suggests obesity-related suppression.

items.question: A child with short stature has bone age equal to height age but delayed relative to chronological age. What does this suggest?
options: Preserved growth potential with possible endocrine delay
items.explanation: Delayed bone age relative to chronological age implies remaining growth potential.
items.reference: Bone age: delay relative to chronological age suggests additional growth potential.

items.question: Which MRI finding is most consistent with congenital MPHD?
options: Pituitary stalk interruption with hypoplastic anterior pituitary
Normal pituitary size with empty sella
items.explanation: Pituitary stalk interruption syndrome is classic for congenital MPHD.
items.reference: MPHD imaging: pituitary stalk interruption and hypoplastic pituitary are classic.

items.statement: Short stature is defined as height below -2 SDS for age and sex.
items.explanation: This is the standard clinical definition.
items.reference: Short stature: height < -2 SDS defines abnormal short stature.

items.statement: A sustained drop in growth velocity can precede a fall in height percentile.
items.explanation: Velocity decline is an early sign of growth failure.
items.reference: Growth velocity: a sustained slowdown precedes percentile crossing.

items.statement: Growth hormone is the main driver of fetal linear growth.
items.explanation: Fetal growth is primarily placental and IGF-2 driven.
items.reference: Fetal growth: placenta and IGF-2 are primary drivers.

items.statement: Obesity can lower GH secretion without true GH deficiency.
items.explanation: Obesity suppresses GH but growth is usually preserved.
items.reference: Obesity-GH paradox: low GH secretion can occur with normal growth.

items.statement: Midparental height provides an estimate of genetic height potential.
items.explanation: It predicts expected adult height range.
items.reference: Target height: midparental height estimates genetic potential.

items.statement: Delayed bone age suggests reduced remaining growth potential.
items.explanation: Delayed bone age indicates more remaining growth potential.
items.reference: Bone age: delay implies additional growth potential.

items.statement: Most small-for-gestational-age infants catch up in growth by age 2 years.
items.explanation: Catch-up is expected in the majority by age 2.
items.reference: SGA: catch-up typically occurs by age 2.

items.statement: Celiac disease can present with isolated short stature without GI symptoms.
items.explanation: Short stature may be the only presenting sign.
items.reference: Celiac disease: isolated short stature may be the presenting feature.

items.statement: Juvenile hypothyroidism typically causes rapid growth and advanced bone age.
items.explanation: It causes growth deceleration and delayed bone age.
items.reference: Juvenile hypothyroidism: slowed growth with delayed bone age.

items.statement: Brachydactyly and obesity are features of pseudohypoparathyroidism.
items.explanation: AHO phenotype includes brachydactyly and obesity.
items.reference: Pseudohypoparathyroidism: AHO features include brachydactyly and obesity.

items.statement: Hip or knee pain in a child on rhGH should prompt evaluation for SCFE.
items.explanation: SCFE risk rises with rapid growth.
items.reference: rhGH safety: hip or knee pain warrants evaluation for SCFE.

items.statement: Neonatal hypoglycemia can be a clue to congenital GHD.
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia is a classic neonatal red flag.
items.reference: Neonatal clues: hypoglycemia suggests congenital GHD/MPHD.

items.assertion: Short stature is defined as height below -2 SDS for age and sex.
items.reason: This corresponds to approximately the 3rd percentile on growth charts.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Definition: height < -2 SDS aligns with the 3rd percentile cutoff.

items.assertion: A persistent decline in growth velocity warrants evaluation even if height percentile is unchanged.
items.reason: Velocity changes can precede percentile crossing.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Growth velocity: slowing is an early marker before percentile shifts.

items.assertion: Endocrine disorders often present after birth rather than in utero.
items.reason: Fetal growth is mainly driven by placental factors and IGF-2.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Physiology: fetal growth is placental/IGF-2 driven, so endocrine causes appear postnatally.

items.assertion: Obese children can have low GH peaks on stimulation testing.
items.reason: Obesity suppresses spontaneous and stimulated GH secretion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Obesity-GH paradox: obesity blunts GH secretion even without true deficiency.

items.assertion: Familial short stature usually has bone age similar to chronological age.
items.reason: Growth timing is normal but genetic height potential is lower.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: FSS: normal timing with bone age near chronological age.

items.assertion: Delayed bone age suggests greater remaining growth potential.
items.reason: Skeletal maturation lags behind chronological age.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone age: delay indicates additional growth potential.

items.assertion: Failure of catch-up growth by age 2 years in SGA infants warrants evaluation.
items.reason: Most SGA infants catch up within the first two years.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: SGA: lack of catch-up by age 2 suggests need for evaluation.

items.assertion: Celiac disease should be considered in children with isolated short stature.
items.reason: Malabsorption and inflammation can impair linear growth without GI symptoms.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Celiac disease: growth failure may be the only manifestation.

items.assertion: Neonatal hypoglycemia can indicate congenital GHD or MPHD.
items.reason: GH and cortisol deficiencies impair counter-regulatory responses.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neonatal clues: hypoglycemia reflects combined GH and cortisol deficiency.

items.assertion: Thyroid function should be monitored after starting rhGH.
items.reason: rhGH can lower free T4 and unmask central hypothyroidism.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: GH therapy: can unmask central hypothyroidism, so thyroid monitoring is required.

items.assertion: Pseudohypoparathyroidism can cause short stature and brachydactyly.
items.reason: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy is associated with these skeletal features.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pseudohypoparathyroidism: AHO phenotype includes short stature and brachydactyly.

items.assertion: Hip pain in a child on rhGH warrants urgent evaluation.
items.reason: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis risk increases with rapid growth.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: rhGH safety: rapid growth increases SCFE risk, so hip pain is urgent.
